# Simulator-Noise Audit of CAR-bench — First-Pass Findings

A study of how much of an agent's measured failure on CAR-bench comes from the
**LLM user-simulator misfiring** rather than from the agent. Generated by
`tools/simulator_audit.py` over 26 local evaluation runs (1,944 trial rows).

## The artifact we measure

On Hallucination and Disambiguation tasks the simulator can end the
conversation on the **first turn** — user message `###STOP###`, the
`HALLUCINATION_ERROR` / `DISAMBIGUATION_ERROR` keyword fires — *before the
agent has produced a single tool call*. The agent is then scored 0 for a
behavior (fabricating, or failing to disambiguate) it was never given the
turns to exhibit. This is unambiguously an evaluation artifact, not agent
behavior: the same agent, on the same task, produces a full correct
conversation on the very next trial.

Detection signature (conservative): category ∈ {hallucination, disambiguation}
∧ first user message == `###STOP###` ∧ agent made 0 tool calls.

## Headline numbers

| Metric | Value |
|---|---|
| Trial rows analysed | 1,944 |
| Failed rows | 409 |
| **Failures that are simulator misfires** | **32 (7.8% of all failures)** |
| 3-trial task-instances that broke Pass^3 | 188 |
| …that would flip to Pass^3-pass without misfires | 8 (4% of Pass^3 breaks) |

## The finding that matters: misfire rate is strongly model-dependent

| User-simulator model | rows | fails | misfires | misfire / fail |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| **Kimi-K2.5** | 63 | 31 | 20 | **65%** |
| GLM-5.2 | 1,878 | 376 | 12 | 3% |
| Gemini-2.5-flash | 3 | 2 | 0 | — (n too small) |

Two-thirds of Kimi-K2.5's "agent failures" are the simulator killing the
conversation on turn one. A direct 3-task comparison confirms it is the
simulator, not the agent: the identical agent scores 3/3 real conversations
under a GLM simulator and 1/3 (two first-turn kills) under MiniMax
(`simtest-glm.json` vs `simtest-minimax.json`).

## Even a "safe" simulator leaves chronically fragile tasks

10 task-instances passed in some trials but misfired in others under the **same
agent** — proof the flip is the simulator. `hallucination_24` is the worst
offender: it flips pass↔misfire across **five independent agent
configurations**, so any Pass^3 score touching it carries simulator variance,
not agent variance.

## Honest scope & caveat (why this is a gift, not an accusation)

Our corpus is dominated by GLM-as-simulator — we switched to it early
*precisely because* Kimi was unsafe — so we have almost no data on the
**official** Gemini-2.5-flash simulator and **cannot claim the official
configuration is affected.** What the data does establish:

1. The misfire phenomenon is real and, on some backends, severe (65%).
2. **Simulator choice is an unmeasured validity axis** — swapping the sim model
   materially changes agent scores, independent of the agent.
3. Even the safe simulator produces reproducibly fragile tasks.

## Proposed fix (≈5 lines in the evaluator)

A **first-turn guard** in `LLMUserSimulationEnv.generate_next_message`: if the
simulator emits an end-conversation error keyword on the first turn — before
the agent has taken any action — re-sample once, or treat as `CONTINUE`. This
removes the artifact without changing legitimate mid-conversation judgments.

## What we hand the organizers

- `tools/simulator_audit.py` — drop-in, consumes the existing run-output JSON,
  no evaluator changes; they can run it on their Gemini configuration and on
  any future simulator swap.
- This quantified report + the fragile-task list.
- The proposed guard.

Framing: simulator choice and determinism are an under-examined axis of
CAR-bench's validity; here is a tool to measure it, evidence that it matters,
a reproducible fragile-task list, and a minimal fix.
